scenario: A FEEL expression must convert a string "true" to a boolean true (e.g., the value came from a form text input where user wrote "true" or "false").
question: Which FEEL built-in fits "string to boolean conversion"?

options.text: A boolean(s) conversion function may or may not exist depending on the specific FEEL implementation version. It is not universally part of the standard FEEL conversion built-ins documented in Camunda 8. The well-documented FEEL conversion functions include number, string, date, time, date and time, and duration. Relying on boolean(s) without verifying it is available in your specific Camunda version risks an evaluation error when the expression runs.

The comparison s = "true" or s = "True" works for those two specific casing variants but fails for other cases like "TRUE" or "True" is already covered, but not "tRuE". A cleaner approach uses lower case(s) = "true", which normalises the string to lowercase first and handles all case variations in a single comparison.

It is accurate that a Checkbox field in a Camunda Form returns a native boolean value and requires no conversion. If the process receives a string-encoded boolean from any source -- whether a form text field, an external API, or a message -- a conversion mechanism is still needed to work with it as a proper boolean in FEEL expressions.

FEEL does not provide a direct boolean(s) constructor for converting a string to a boolean value. Unlike number(s), string(n), date(s), and time(s), the boolean conversion is not a named built-in in the standard FEEL specification. The canonical workaround uses explicit comparison semantics: =s = "true" returns a boolean by evaluating the equality. For case-insensitive handling (users may type "True" or "TRUE"), use =lower case(s) = "true", which first normalises the string to lowercase before comparing. Best practice: avoid string-encoded booleans by using Checkbox or Toggle form fields that return native booleans.

a.text: Incorrect. FEEL's conversion built-ins include number(s), string(n), date(s), time(s), date and time(s), duration(s) -- well-known and documented. A bare boolean(s) conversion isn't part of the standard FEEL spec in the same way. The practical recourse is explicit comparison, which returns boolean by FEEL semantics: =s = "true" -- returns true if s equals "true", false otherwise. =lower case(s) = "true" -- case-insensitive comparison (recommended; users might type "True" or "TRUE"). Best practice: avoid string-encoded booleans from user input in the first place. Use a Checkbox or Toggle field in the Form (which returns a real boolean). Use string-to-boolean conversion only when the data source forces it.

b.text: Incorrect. Some FEEL implementations may have added it; not safe to rely on without checking.

c.text: Incorrect. lower case(s) = "true" covers case variations.

d.text: Correct. Checkbox returns boolean; Text Input doesn't.

explanation: Checkbox returns boolean; Text Input doesn't.
